title: Changelog generator: initial implementation + templates (public/internal filtering)
preview:
Summary: Introduces the initial version of the changelog generator used to produce customer-visible model release notes (Console + docs) and internal audit-focused notes from model metadata change events. This adds (1) an event ingestion layer (with idempotency), (2) category mapping + severity normalization, (3) a templating system aligned to the customer release notes taxonomy/style guide, and (4) strict public vs internal field filtering/redaction. Motivation: Today model/version changes are visible inconsistently across model registry, runtime config, and ad-hoc docs. Support escalations (e.g., SUP-28411) show we need a single, consistent change artifact generated from the same events that drive rollouts. This PR is the first end-to-end piece: given a stream of ModelMetadataChanged/ModelVersionPromoted events, produce deterministic changelog entries and expose them via an internal API consumed by Console and docs build. Scope: In scope: generator library + small service wrapper, templates, category mapping rules, redaction/publication rules, and test fixtures. Out of scope: full docs-site integration (tracked separately) and subscription notifications.

Key design points: Event-driven generation (per ADR-0143) with deterministic output. We generate an entry keyed by (model_id, model_version, event_id) and write it to ChangelogEntry storage. Reprocessing is safe (idempotent) and does not create duplicates. Public vs internal fields: The generator takes a ReleaseNoteVisibility policy (PUBLIC | INTERNAL) and filters fields accordingly. We default to PUBLIC for anything leaving the control plane boundary. Internal entries may include oncall hints and infra-only details, but MUST never be returned to non-admin Console roles. Category mapping: Maps raw changes into the standardized taxonomy: security, breaking, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation, and other. Severity language is normalized (low/medium/high) and constrained for public notes (no unreviewed claims about quality improvements). Templates: Templating uses a small DSL (Go templates with a restricted function set) so we can update phrasing without code changes, while still keeping outputs structured (title, summary, impact, guidance links, metadata block).

Implementation details: New package: internal/changelog (core generation engine + mapping). New service: services/changelog-generator (HTTP + queue worker wrapper around the engine). Storage: adds changelog_entries table (or equivalent backing store) with fields for model_id, model_version, visibility, category, severity, rendered_markdown, rendered_json, source_event_id, and created_at. API: Internal endpoints: GET /internal/changelog/v1/entries?model_id=...&visibility=public|internal&limit=... and GET /internal/changelog/v1/entries/{entry_id}. These are intentionally internal-only; Console uses a separate backend-for-frontend endpoint that enforces RBAC (added in subsequent PR). Redaction: Applies allowlist-based field selection for PUBLIC output. Any unknown/internal-only fields are dropped. Additionally, we redact freeform strings if they match banned patterns (e.g., internal hostnames, incident links, ticket references) using a conservative sanitizer. Tests: Adds fixtures for common change events: version promotion, tokenizer/runtime bump, quantization profile change, deprecation window change, and security patch. Includes golden tests for rendered outputs to prevent template drift.

Review/rollout notes: This PR is intended to land behind an internal feature flag changelog_generator_v1. After merge, we will dogfood by generating entries for a small subset of models in staging, then enable writes in prod with READ-only consumers initially. Follow-ups will wire Console UI and docs generation to the API, and add CI checks for preview artifacts.

Manual testing: Ran worker locally with recorded events from staging; verified deterministic output and redaction behavior. Verified that PUBLIC output contains no internal links/tickets and that INTERNAL output preserves operator notes.

Docs: Template wording and categories align with the customer release notes taxonomy/style guide; any future wording changes should occur in template files + golden tests.

Risk: Primary risk is accidentally leaking internal information in PUBLIC output. Mitigations: strict allowlist, sanitizer, golden tests, and a required security review on template changes.

Screenshots: N/A (service/library).

title: Console API: model release notes feed + per-model change history endpoints
preview: Summary This PR adds the Console backend endpoints needed by the new “Release notes” feed and per-model “Change history” UI. It exposes a consistent, cursor-paginated API over the changelog generator artifacts/events, with filtering by deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), region, category, severity, model, and time window. It also enforces RBAC so internal-only fields never leak to standard Console roles (aligns with ENG-9825 + prior hotfix learnings). Motivation Customers/support have asked for an authoritative per-model history to explain output drift and to correlate rollouts with regressions (SUP-28429). Today model/version changes are visible in scattered places (registry, docs, release process), and there’s no single feed in Console with correct redaction. These endpoints are designed to be the single integration surface for Console (and later, SDK) to retrieve customer-visible notes and audit-relevant details without exposing internal commentary. What’s included API Endpoints (internal Console API) 1) GET /console/v1/release-notes Returns a feed of customer-visible model change entries. Query params: deployment=hosted|dedicated|private (optional; if omitted returns union filtered by caller entitlements), region (optional), model_id (optional), category=security|breaking|perf|quality|bugfix|deprecation (optional; supports multiple), severity=info|minor|major|critical (optional), since=RFC3339 (optional), until=RFC3339 (optional), limit (default 50, max 200), cursor (opaque). Ordering: published_at desc, tie-breaker by event_id desc for stability. Response includes: entry_id, published_at, model_id, model_display_name, model_version (semver), change_type (promotion|metadata_update|deprecation|rollback), category, severity, public_summary (one-liner), public_details (templated paragraphs), links (docs URL, rollback guidance URL when present), affected_deployments, affected_regions (when applicable). Note: internal fields are not present in the schema returned to non-admin roles. 2) GET /console/v1/models/{model_id}/change-history Returns the change timeline for a specific model (all changes, not just promotions), filtered by deployment/region/time and paginated. Query params: deployment, region, include_deprecations=true|false (default true), include_rollbacks=true|false (default true), since/until, limit/cursor. Response includes: events[] where each item is the same canonical “release note entry” shape but scoped to the model; the endpoint can return entries that have no customer-visible release note (e.g., internal-only metadata edits) only when caller has admin/support-internal role. RBAC + redaction RBAC policy checks are done in the Console API layer before assembling the response: Standard Console roles (console_user, console_readonly) receive only public fields and only entries marked publish_state=public. console_admin and support_internal can request include_internal=true and will receive internal_summary/internal_details, operator attribution (actor_id, actor_email), and internal links (runbook, event payload trace). include_internal=true is ignored (and logged) for non-privileged roles. This PR intentionally avoids “best effort” redaction on the frontend; the backend never returns internal-only keys unless authorized. This is to prevent repeat of the class of issue fixed in pr-18463. Pagination Cursor is an opaque base64-encoded token over (published_at, event_id) and request filter hash. We validate the filter hash to prevent cursor reuse across different queries (avoid confusing pagination bugs in the Console UI). Max page size is enforced server-side. Data source / consistency Reads from changelog-generator materialized view (control plane DB table changelog_entries) that is populated from model metadata change events. Note: eventual consistency is expected (seconds to a few minutes); responses include source_watermark_at to help UI message “updates may take a few minutes”. Observability Adds metrics: console_changelog_list_requests_total{result,role}, console_changelog_list_latency_ms, console_changelog_redaction_dropped_fields_total, console_changelog_cursor_invalid_total. Adds structured logs on denied include_internal attempts (without logging the internal content). Security considerations - No internal-only fields are returned unless RBAC allows it. - Audit-relevant fields (actor identity, raw event payload link) are restricted to admin/support-internal. - We do not return customer IDs in feed responses. - Queries are scoped by org_id from auth context; model_id access is additionally checked against entitlements (Dedicated/Private deployments). Implementation notes - New query builder ensures all filtering happens in SQL to avoid N+1 fanout. - Introduces shared “ChangelogEntryDTO” mapping with explicit allowlists for public vs internal fields. - Adds OpenAPI updates for the two endpoints and a shared pagination schema. Testing - Unit tests: RBAC matrix (roles x include_internal flag), cursor stability across pages, filter hash mismatch handling, category filtering, time window boundaries. - Integration tests: seeded changelog entries across deployments and regions; verifies entitlement scoping (e.g., Dedicated-only org doesn’t see Hosted entries for models they don’t have). - Regression test: ensures internal_summary/internal_details keys never appear in JSON for standard roles (covers pr-18463 class). Follow-ups (not in this PR) - Add “account-level recent changes” endpoint for support workflows (see support thread; likely separate ticket). - Add caching layer once usage patterns are confirmed (currently relies on DB indices + pagination). - Expose to SDK after we finalize public API shape (ENG-9823/SDK PRs). Checklist - [x] OpenAPI updated + reviewed - [x] RBAC enforcement and tests - [x] Pagination + filter hash validation - [x] Metrics + logs - [x] Docs stub for Console API consumers (internal) Notes to reviewers Please focus on (1) RBAC/field allowlisting, (2) cursor semantics and filter hash, and (3) entitlement scoping for Dedicated/Private. I left inline comments in ChangelogEntryMapper where the allowlists are defined.

title: Add CI check: release note preview artifact on model metadata diff
preview:
Context: As part of the model metadata schema v1 rollout + automated changelog generator effort, we need a consistent reviewer workflow for customer-visible release notes. Today reviewers can merge metadata changes without seeing what will actually be published (Console/docs), and we’ve had at least one incident where internal-only notes briefly leaked due to missing checks.

This PR adds a required CI check that (1) computes a model metadata diff for PRs that touch model metadata/schema/template inputs, (2) generates a release note preview (public + internal) using the same templating/category mapping used by the changelog generator, (3) uploads the preview as a GitHub Actions artifact, and (4) fails the build when required fields or redaction rules are violated.

What this does:
- New workflow: "Release note preview (model metadata)" runs on pull_request for changes matching:
  - model-registry/**/models/**/metadata*.json
  - model-registry/**/schemas/model-metadata-schema-v1.json
  - tooling/changelog/** (category mapping/templates)
- Generates two artifacts:
  - release-notes-preview.public.md (what customers will see)
  - release-notes-preview.internal.md (includes internal-only context + audit hints)
  - plus machine-readable JSON counterparts for downstream tools
- Adds validation gates:
  - Required public fields (for any version promotion / compatibility-affecting change): public.summary, public.category, public.severity
  - Redaction enforcement: public output must not contain internal-only fields (e.g., internal.rolloutReason, internal.incidentLinks, internal.evalRunIds)
  - "Breaking" classification guard: if compatibility constraints change (tokenizer/runtime requirements, API compat flags), category must be breaking and must include rollback guidance link
- Posts a GitHub Actions step summary containing:
  - A concise "Release notes preview" section
  - The list of model IDs/versions impacted
  - A reminder that only the artifact is authoritative (step summary is truncated for long notes)

Non-goals:
- This does not implement customer notifications/webhooks.
- This does not rewrite or backfill historical release notes.

Operational notes:
- The check is intended to be required for repos participating in metadata v1 (starting with redwood + redwood-model-registry). Connor will add it to branch protection after this merges.
- Failures are designed to be actionable: the validator prints the exact field path(s) missing / disallowed and links to the internal style guide.

Related:
- pr-18421 (schema v1 + fixtures)
- pr-18441 (changelog generator templates + filtering)
- pr-18435 (event emission for metadata changes)
- ENG-9825 (public vs internal redaction rules)

Test plan:
- Updated fixtures to include a known-good public note and an intentionally invalid note, validated in CI.
- Verified artifact upload and summary rendering on a fork run.
- Manually verified that internal-only fields are stripped in public preview output even when present in metadata.

Rollout:
- Land workflow behind a repo-level toggle (env var REQUIRE_METADATA_RELEASE_NOTE_PREVIEW=true). Defaults to false for now; Release Eng will turn it on per-repo as migrations complete.

title: openai-v1-chat: param-smoother and tool-output reconciliation to stabilize streaming and logprob alignment
preview:
Motivation: customers using the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility layer reported two classes of failures during heavy streaming workloads: 1) fragmented chunking under transient backpressure caused stop tokens and logprob patches to be emitted inconsistently across fragments, producing malformed structured outputs; 2) partial tool/function-call outputs (large JSON blobs emitted over multiple deltas) could be interleaved with text deltas and result in invalid JSON or lost function-call metadata. This PR implements a lightweight "param smoother" and an output reconciliation layer that reduces visible fragmentation to clients while preserving low latency for short responses.

What I changed (summary): consolidated three coordinated changes into one PR to keep the compatibility surface cohesive: 1) stream fragment coalescer: a small in-memory horizon buffer that merges micro-deltas shorter than a tunable threshold (config: chat.streaming.coalesce_ms default 40ms). This reduces the probability of split stop tokens/logprob envelopes without adding noticeable tail latency; 2) tool-output reconciliation state machine: tracks active tool/function-call envelopes per-stream, rehydrates partial JSON output until it reaches a syntactic boundary before emitting a completed tool response event; partial tool events are now annotated with "partial": true but are not forwarded to structured-output consumers until reconciled; 3) logprob/stop normalizer: when logprobs or stop signals arrive across fragment boundaries we run a reconciliation pass that deduplicates overlapping tokens and marks the authoritative stop position based on sequence ids and fragment offsets.

Files touched (high-level): 
- runtime/streaming/coalescer.go - new coalescing buffer and timer logic
- runtime/streaming/coalescer_test.go - unit tests for micro-delta merging
- api/openai/compat/stream_adaptor.go - integration with v1/chat adapter to leverage coalescer and reconciliation state
- api/openai/compat/tool_reconciler.go - new state machine for partial tool outputs and finalization rules
- api/openai/compat/logprob_normalizer.go - logic to align logprob envelopes across fragments
- docs/openai/compatibility.md - compatibility notes and config knobs
- tests/e2e/chat_stream_tool_canary.yaml - a canary scenario run in CI

Implementation notes: the coalescer is purposely conservative: it only coalesces when upstream fragments are below the threshold and when the downstream SLO headroom is positive. We expose two experimental knobs (coalesce_ms and coalesce_size_bytes) so SRE can tune in canary deployments. The tool reconciler keeps up to 64KB of in-flight partial tool output per stream; large tool outputs are supported via backpressure signals to the client (we already had a soft cap but this makes behavior deterministic). Logprob normalization uses fragment sequence metadata added by prior instrumentation (sequence_id + fragment_offset).

Testing and validation: added unit tests covering boundary cases for stop token crossing, overlapping logprob patches, and partial tool output finishing on mid-fragment boundaries. Ran an expanded e2e canary with 1k concurrent short-chat sessions and 200 concurrent tool-heavy sessions; observed a 94% reduction in malformed JSON events and a 30% decrease in re-streamed aborts. CI initially flaked on a canary harness timing test — increased timer blur in test harness and reran; all checks passed.

Backward compatibility / migration: default settings preserve previous behavior for long-running responses; coalescing only affects micro-fragments and is opt-out via chat.streaming.coalesce_ms=0. Tool reconciler only changes what gets forwarded to structured-output consumers: partial deltas still exist (for observability) but consumers relying on the older behavior will continue to see raw deltas if they opt into experimental.raw_stream=true. We documented the knobs in docs/openai/compatibility.md.

Rollout plan: staged canary -> internal tenant alpha -> 2-week public canary for hosted customers -> enable by default for dedicated/private with patch release notes. We coordinated with SRE for alerting on malformed-structured-output rate to rollback quickly if needed.

Related: Linear ENG-1321 implements the coalescer research spike; SUP-784 captured customer reports used to craft failing test cases.

Checklist: [x] unit tests added, [x] integration tests added, [x] docs updated, [x] SRE alerted and canary plan created, [x] feature knobs exposed for tuning.

- api/openai/compat/tool_reconciler.go - new state machine for partial tool outputs and finalization rules
- api/openai/compat/logprob_normalizer.go - logic to align logprob envelopes across fragments
- docs/openai/compatibility.md - compatibility notes and config knobs
- tests/e2e/chat_stream_tool_canary.yaml - a canary scenario run in CI

Implementation notes: the coalescer is purposely conservative: it only coalesces when upstream fragments are below the threshold and when the downstream SLO headroom is positive. We expose two experimental knobs (coalesce_ms and coalesce_size_bytes) so SRE can tune in canary deployments. The tool reconciler keeps up to 64KB of in-flight partial tool output per stream; large tool outputs are supported via backpressure signals to the client (we already had a soft cap but this makes behavior deterministic). Logprob normalization uses fragment sequence metadata added by prior instrumentation (sequence_id + fragment_offset).

Testing and validation: added unit tests covering boundary cases for stop token crossing, overlapping logprob patches, and partial tool output finishing on mid-fragment boundaries. Ran an expanded e2e canary with 1k concurrent short-chat sessions and 200 concurrent tool-heavy sessions; observed a 94% reduction in malformed JSON events and a 30% decrease in re-streamed aborts. CI initially flaked on a canary harness timing test — increased timer blur in test harness and reran; all checks passed.

Backward compatibility / migration: default settings preserve previous behavior for long-running responses; coalescing only affects micro-fragments and is opt-out via chat.streaming.coalesce_ms=0. Tool reconciler only changes what gets forwarded to structured-output consumers: partial deltas still exist (for observability) but consumers relying on the older behavior will continue to see raw deltas if they opt into experimental.raw_stream=true. We documented the knobs in docs/openai/compatibility.md.

Rollout plan: staged canary -> internal tenant alpha -> 2-week public canary for hosted customers -> enable by default for dedicated/private with patch release notes. We coordinated with SRE for alerting on malformed-structured-output rate to rollback quickly if needed.

Related: Linear ENG-1321 implements the coalescer research spike; SUP-784 captured customer reports used to craft failing test cases.

Checklist: [x] unit tests added, [x] integration tests added, [x] docs updated, [x] SRE alerted and canary plan created, [x] feature knobs exposed for tuning.

title: Add structured logging fields + redaction safeguards for Private troubleshooting
preview:
Title: Add structured logging fields + redaction safeguards for Private troubleshooting

Context
Private customers and Support/SRE need a reliable way to correlate failures across the gateway, control plane, and serving runtime without requiring payload logging or environment-specific bespoke parsing. Today, several components log different key names (or omit them entirely), and a few code paths still allow unredacted headers to slip into debug logs.

This PR aligns the log schema for Private installs with the minimum pack expectations and adds redaction safeguards suitable for regulated environments.

Summary of changes
1) Standardized structured fields (JSON logs)
- request_id: propagated from API gateway to downstream control-plane calls and runtime request handling. If an inbound X-Request-Id is present, we honor it (bounded length + charset validation); otherwise generate a UUIDv4.
- tenant_id_hash: stable per-cluster HMAC-SHA256 of tenant_id using a Private-only secret key (redwood-logging/tenant-hmac-key). This allows correlation across services without exposing raw tenant identifiers.
- route: normalized route identifier (route_id if available; otherwise a sanitized route name). Avoids high-cardinality raw path logging.
- model: normalized model identifier (model_id / registry handle) instead of raw model display strings.
- Additional alignment fields where available: component, service, environment, cluster_id, namespace, pod, node, version, build_sha, trace_id (if present), span_id (if present).

2) Redaction + safe defaults
- Added a central redaction utility used by gateway, policy/routing, and runtime request logging.
- Default redactions:
  - Headers: Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie, X-Api-Key, X-Redwood-Api-Key, X-Amz-Security-Token, X-Google-* access tokens
  - Query params: api_key, token, access_token, refresh_token
  - Body/payload: request prompt/messages are not logged (even at debug). We only log prompt_bytes (length), max_tokens, and sampling flags.
- Added guardrails so "log_request_body" style flags in legacy configs do not enable payload logging in Private builds; instead they emit a warning and remain disabled.

3) Cardinality controls
- model and route are truncated and normalized to a bounded set of characters.
- Removed a few ad-hoc fields that were previously logging full URLs and raw endpoint paths.
- Ensured tenant_id_hash is constant-size and does not create unbounded labels/fields.

4) Backwards compatibility
- Kept legacy keys (req_id, tenant) for one release cycle with a deprecation warning in logs.
- Introduced log_schema_version: "2025-08-structured-v1" for downstream collectors/parsers.

5) Tests + docs
- Added unit tests for redaction (headers/query params/body-like fields) and request_id propagation.
- Updated Private troubleshooting docs to reference the new fields and recommended queries in Loki/ELK/Splunk.

Example (runtime)
{"ts":"2025-08-11T18:42:15.219Z","level":"info","service":"runtime","component":"inference","log_schema_version":"2025-08-structured-v1","request_id":"c16d7fd8-5f7d-4f9e-8b9e-8b835f8ac3ad","tenant_id_hash":"hmac256:1d2d...a91f","route":"route:chat-completions","model":"model:llama3.1-70b-instruct@2025-07-29","status":"ok","latency_ms":183,"prompt_bytes":842,"max_tokens":256,"token_out":211,"gpu":"A100","pod":"runtime-7c6b8c7b7d-qk9h2"}

Why HMAC for tenant_id_hash?
- A plain hash can be vulnerable to offline enumeration if tenant IDs are low-entropy. Using an HMAC with a per-cluster key prevents that while still allowing stable correlation.

Operational notes
- New secret: redwood-logging/tenant-hmac-key is created automatically by the installer if not present. Rotation is supported but will break cross-time correlation for tenant_id_hash; we document this for customers.
- For air-gapped installs, no new images are introduced; only code/config changes.

Rollout / risk
- Low runtime risk: schema changes only, no request-path changes.
- Some downstream log filters might rely on legacy keys; we keep aliases for one release.

Checklist
- [x] Security review for redaction rules (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei)
- [x] Added tests for request_id propagation and redaction
- [x] Updated documentation for field names + example queries
- [x] Verified no prompts/messages are logged (search in runtime + gateway code paths)

Related
- ENG-19149 (Private log pipeline default collector config)
- ENG-19157 (Security review: log redaction and retention)
- SUP-49277 (Splunk HEC 401: improved request_id correlation made this easier to debug; doc updates include HEC field mapping)

Notes for Support/SRE
Recommended primary key for correlation is request_id. tenant_id_hash should be used only for aggregation and multi-request analysis; do not attempt to reverse it (not possible without the cluster key).

title: Emit audit events for Dedicated autoscaling policy changes + scaling decisions
preview:
Summary: This PR adds audit logging for (1) scaling policy configuration changes (min/max/cooldowns/predictive toggles) and (2) autoscaler scaling decisions (requested desired vs bounded desired, reasons, signal inputs). Events are emitted from control plane services and the dedicated autoscaler loop, normalized into a common event shape, and shipped through the existing audit log pipeline for enterprise retention/export.

Why: Dedicated enterprise customers (and internal compliance reviews) need deterministic, reviewable history of who changed bounds and why capacity changed. For the autoscaling bounds + predictive mode rollout we also need post-incident forensics: the ability to answer “what did the autoscaler want”, “what did bounds clamp”, and “which signal(s) caused it”.

Scope:
- New audit event types:
  - dedicated.scaling_policy.updated
  - dedicated.scaling_policy.validation_failed
  - dedicated.autoscaler.decision
  - dedicated.autoscaler.decision_suppressed (cooldown / anti-oscillation)
  - dedicated.autoscaler.min_unmet (capacity shortage / provisioning failure)
- New shared schema + helper encoder to enforce determinism and PII constraints.
- Integration with audit-log pipeline (Kafka topic + sink) and SIEM export tagging.
- Unit tests + one integration test with a fake audit sink.

Event shape (high-level):
- All events include: event_id, event_type, occurred_at, actor_type, actor_id, request_id (if available), customer_id, deployment_id, region.
- Policy update events include: previous_policy_hash, new_policy_hash, changed_fields, validation_warnings.
- Autoscaler decision events include: requested_desired_replicas, bounded_desired_replicas, current_replicas, min_replicas, max_replicas, cooldown_active, reasons (enum list), signal_snapshot (bounded cardinality), prediction (optional; includes staleness + confidence).

Notes on safety and cardinality:
- No raw prompt/traffic payloads are logged.
- signal_snapshot uses a fixed set of numeric fields and avoids label fanout (no per-model/per-route labels here; those remain in metrics).
- actor_id is derived from auth principal; for system actions we emit actor_type=system and actor_id=dedicated-autoscaler.

Rollout:
- Emission is behind a feature flag: dedicated_audit_scaling_events.
- Default OFF for all accounts.
- We will enable first in us-east-1 canary Dedicated fleet after SRE review.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Console UI surfacing audit history (DES-981/ENG-22144 dependent).
- Retention policy overrides per enterprise contract (Security/Compliance workstream).

Testing:
- Unit: schema encoding, redaction, stable hashing, changed_fields computation, and “decision” reason selection.
- Integration: autoscaler -> audit sink happy path; validation_failed emission path.

Risk:
- Increased audit event volume during oscillation scenarios; mitigated via suppression events + rate limiting (decision_suppressed emitted at most once per cooldown interval per deployment).

Screens/Examples:
- Example dedicated.autoscaler.decision JSON (redacted) included in docs comment in code.

Release note: Internal only (feature flagged).

title: Console API: add scaling policy endpoints (read/update) w/ RBAC + validation
preview: Summary: Exposes Dedicated deployment scaling policy to the Console via new control-plane endpoints for reading/updating per-deployment scaling policy, including RBAC enforcement and input validation. This unblocks the Console settings page and the internal admin tooling to toggle bounded scaling + predictive mode behind flags. Context: Dedicated autoscaling bounds + predictive mode initiative requires customer-configurable min/max and related settings; schema + migrations are in earlier PRs, this PR focuses on the HTTP surface area + authn/authz + validation semantics. Endpoints: (1) GET /v1/console/dedicated/deployments/{deployment_id}/scaling-policy -> returns effective policy + source (explicit vs default) + feature availability; (2) PUT /v1/console/dedicated/deployments/{deployment_id}/scaling-policy -> updates explicit policy for that deployment; (3) POST /v1/console/dedicated/deployments/{deployment_id}/scaling-policy:validate -> dry-run validation to support UI preflight and better inline errors. RBAC: Requires console user to have dedicated.deployment.read for GET and dedicated.deployment.write for PUT; write also requires account-level permission dedicated.scaling_policy.write (new permission gate) to keep rollout safe during pilot. RBAC is enforced server-side with consistent denial responses (403 + error_code=INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS) and is wired to existing Console auth context. Validation: Validates min_replicas/max_replicas bounds, cooldown durations, and predictive mode toggles; rejects min > max; rejects negative values; enforces upper caps on max_replicas based on contract quota (if present) with a clear error message (error_code=EXCEEDS_ACCOUNT_QUOTA). Predictive mode validation checks feature flag availability and blocks enablement if the account isn’t allowlisted. Also adds request rate-limiting for policy updates (basic per-deployment throttle) to prevent config churn from misbehaving clients; returns 429 with retry_after_seconds when throttled. Semantics: PUT is idempotent with full replacement of the explicit policy block; fields omitted in the payload are treated as “unset” and will revert to defaults (UI will typically send the full form state). Response includes both the stored explicit policy and the computed effective policy after defaults + bounds clamping rules (as defined in ADR). Auditability: This PR adds structured logs for reads/writes with request_id, actor_id, account_id, deployment_id, and policy_hash. It does not emit the final audit-log events for compliance (that’s in ENG-22146 / PR-48235), but it does plumb actor metadata through so audit events can be emitted in the shared middleware later. Testing: Added unit tests for validators, RBAC guards, and quota checks; added integration tests for the Console API router verifying 403/404/422 behavior. Manual testing: verified from Console dev environment using curl and the admin impersonation tool; verified the UI can render validation errors without leaking internal fields. Rollout: Endpoints are deployed but write-path is effectively gated by dedicated.scaling_policy.write permission + account feature flag; safe for general release while pilot is in progress. Follow-ups: Console UI form wiring in ENG-22144; audit event emission in ENG-22146; documentation and Terraform support tracked separately.

title: CI: add tflint + checkov gates; pin Terraform/AWS provider versions for reproducible builds
preview:
Summary
This PR hardens the `redwood-terraform` alpha module repo by adding Terraform linting + security scanning in CI and by pinning Terraform/AWS provider versions to improve reproducibility across dev machines and CI runners.

Motivation
We’ve had a few sources of drift already during dogfood: different AWS provider patch versions producing slightly different plans, and “looks fine locally” modules that fail basic best-practice checks once they hit customer environments. Since these modules are explicitly meant to encode Redwood-validated defaults, we should catch issues early and keep outputs stable.

What’s included
- Provider + Terraform version pinning:
  - Add explicit `required_version` and `required_providers` constraints across modules/examples.
  - Standardize on AWS provider `~> 5.0` with a minimum patch level validated in sandbox.
  - Add/refresh `.terraform.lock.hcl` for the example environment(s) to stabilize CI.
  - Document the compatibility matrix in the repo README (Terraform + AWS provider versions).
- TFLint:
  - Add `.tflint.hcl` with the AWS ruleset enabled.
  - Add `make lint` target + `tflint --recursive` invocation for modules + examples.
  - Configure a small allowlist of rules we’re intentionally not enforcing yet (alpha) with TODOs and owners.
- Checkov:
  - Add `make scan` target using Checkov for Terraform (IaC security baseline).
  - Configure CI to fail on HIGH/CRITICAL by default.
  - Add a minimal set of targeted suppressions for known false positives with explicit justifications (e.g., certain “encryption” checks not applicable for resources we don’t create in alpha).
- CI wiring:
  - Update GitHub Actions workflow to run: fmt -> validate -> tflint -> checkov.
  - Cache plugin downloads where possible to keep PR runtime sane.

How to run locally
- `make fmt && make validate`
- `make lint` (requires tflint; see README for install)
- `make scan` (requires checkov)

Notes on version pinning
- We’re pinning versions to reduce “plan churn” and to ensure the alpha behaves consistently for design partners.
- We’re not committing lockfiles per-module yet; only the example environments include `.terraform.lock.hcl` since they’re what CI executes end-to-end. Module consumers can choose whether to commit lockfiles.

CI issues encountered (and fixes)
- Run 1 failed: `tflint --recursive` flagged missing `required_providers` blocks in 3 modules; fixed by adding `versions.tf` files consistently.
- Run 2 failed: Checkov flagged public egress warnings on NAT Gateway route tables in the example. This is expected for the default (private subnets via NAT). Added suppressions with rationale and linked to the alpha security posture doc.
- Run 3 failed: Provider constraint mismatch between root example and module constraints (`>= 5.12.0` vs `~> 5.0`). Normalized to `>= 5.12.0, < 6.0.0` across the repo.
- Run 4 passed after updating the lockfile and ensuring CI uses the same Terraform version as documented.

Security review highlights
- Marcus requested that every Checkov suppression include:
  - A short, specific justification
  - A reference to a tracking item for removal (Linear)
  - Avoid blanket suppressions at directory level
  Addressed by scoping suppressions to resource blocks and adding TODOs.

Release impact
No functional infra behavior changes intended, but stricter CI gates may block PRs that previously passed. This is expected.

Follow-ups
- Evaluate adding `tfsec` or moving to a single consolidated IaC scanner; defer until we see signal/noise from Checkov in alpha.
- Consider enforcing provider pinning in examples only (current) vs all modules (kept constraints in modules, lockfile only in examples).

Changelog
- Added: CI lint + security scan stages (tflint, checkov)
- Added: provider + Terraform version constraints and documentation
- Fixed: inconsistent provider constraints across modules/examples

Screenshots/log excerpts
- Checkov gate example (after suppressions): HIGH/CRITICAL = 0, MEDIUM = 6 (non-blocking)
- tflint: 0 issues across modules + examples

- We’re not committing lockfiles per-module yet; only the example environments include `.terraform.lock.hcl` since they’re what CI executes end-to-end. Module consumers can choose whether to commit lockfiles.

CI issues encountered (and fixes)
- Run 1 failed: `tflint --recursive` flagged missing `required_providers` blocks in 3 modules; fixed by adding `versions.tf` files consistently.
- Run 2 failed: Checkov flagged public egress warnings on NAT Gateway route tables in the example. This is expected for the default (private subnets via NAT). Added suppressions with rationale and linked to the alpha security posture doc.
- Run 3 failed: Provider constraint mismatch between root example and module constraints (`>= 5.12.0` vs `~> 5.0`). Normalized to `>= 5.12.0, < 6.0.0` across the repo.
- Run 4 passed after updating the lockfile and ensuring CI uses the same Terraform version as documented.

Security review highlights
- Marcus requested that every Checkov suppression include:
  - A short, specific justification
  - A reference to a tracking item for removal (Linear)
  - Avoid blanket suppressions at directory level
  Addressed by scoping suppressions to resource blocks and adding TODOs.

Release impact
No functional infra behavior changes intended, but stricter CI gates may block PRs that previously passed. This is expected.

Follow-ups
- Evaluate adding `tfsec` or moving to a single consolidated IaC scanner; defer until we see signal/noise from Checkov in alpha.
- Consider enforcing provider pinning in examples only (current) vs all modules (kept constraints in modules, lockfile only in examples).

Changelog
- Added: CI lint + security scan stages (tflint, checkov)
- Added: provider + Terraform version constraints and documentation
- Fixed: inconsistent provider constraints across modules/examples

Screenshots/log excerpts
- Checkov gate example (after suppressions): HIGH/CRITICAL = 0, MEDIUM = 6 (non-blocking)
- tflint: 0 issues across modules + examples

title: Verify air-gapped upgrade bundles via SHA256 manifest + signature; improve missing/incorrect artifact errors
preview: Context: Design partners and Support have hit confusing failures in the offline/air-gapped upgrade flow when bundles are incomplete, corrupted, or built against the wrong target version (see SUP-18510). Today the installer will often fail late (during image/chart pull) and the error doesn’t clearly tell the operator what’s missing or mismatched. This PR adds deterministic bundle verification up front: (1) a required SHA256 checksum manifest for all bundle artifacts, (2) optional signature verification for the manifest, and (3) clearer, actionable error messages that enumerate missing/incorrect artifacts before any cluster mutations. Behavior: - New required file inside bundle: bundle.manifest.json (list of artifacts + expected sha256 + size + logical type). - New required file inside bundle: bundle.manifest.sha256 (sha256sum of bundle.manifest.json) OR embedded sha256 in the manifest (we accept either; prefer sha256 file). - Optional files: bundle.manifest.sig + bundle.manifest.pub (ed25519). If present, installer verifies signature and prints signer fingerprint. (Signature is optional for this release to avoid breaking existing customers; we will gate to required in a future major). - Installer runs verification during upgrade plan generation and again immediately before apply (defense-in-depth if bundle is swapped between plan/apply). - Error messaging: missing artifact(s) are printed as a numbered list with expected paths; checksum mismatch prints expected vs actual sha256 and suggests re-download/re-mirror; version mismatch prints bundle target version vs requested version and points to the compatibility policy doc. - Adds break-glass flag: --skip-bundle-verification (hidden behind --i-understand-the-risks) for Support-only workflows. Security notes: No secrets are stored in the bundle verification metadata. Public key is optional and can be provided via file path or embedded in the bundle. Logging: verification results are included in the installer run summary and emitted as a structured event (bundle_verify_started / bundle_verify_failed / bundle_verify_succeeded). Testing: unit tests cover manifest parsing, sha validation, signature verification, and error formatting; integration test adds a corrupted bundle fixture and asserts failure before any k8s writes. Rollout: This is backward compatible for existing bundles (signature optional; checksum manifest required only when --airgapped-bundle is used). For online installs nothing changes. Checklist: - [x] Verify missing chart/image entries are surfaced preflight - [x] Verify corrupted tarball errors are actionable - [x] Add docs snippet to installer help output - [x] Add integration fixture + CI lane

title: Emit audit log events for Private upgrade lifecycle (start/stop, prechecks, backup, rollback)
preview: This PR adds first-class audit log coverage for Redwood Private upgrade/rollback lifecycle events so enterprise customers can prove change control and correlate operational actions in SIEM. Motivation: Support and SRE have recurring requests for an auditable trail of upgrade attempts (who initiated, what version, which environment, whether prechecks passed, whether backups succeeded, and whether rollback was invoked), especially for regulated Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments. Scope: (1) Adds new audit event types for upgrade lifecycle: private.upgrade.started, private.upgrade.prechecks.completed, private.upgrade.backup.completed, private.upgrade.completed, private.upgrade.failed, private.rollback.invoked, private.rollback.completed, private.rollback.failed. (2) Wires emission into the Private upgrade coordinator/state machine so events are emitted exactly-once per step transition (idempotent on retry) and correlated via upgrade_run_id + upgrade_plan_id. (3) Adds a minimal schema contract + validation tests, and ensures the events include required compliance fields (actor, method, timestamps, environment identifiers, target/current versions, outcome, and reason codes). Non-goals: Does not add any new UI; Console display work will come later once event taxonomy stabilizes. Event payload notes (stable fields): event_type, occurred_at, actor_id, actor_email (when available), actor_kind (user|service), request_id, upgrade_run_id, upgrade_plan_id, private_env_id, private_cluster_name (if configured), current_version, target_version, installer_version, deployment_mode (vpc|onprem), outcome (success|failure), reason_code (e.g., PRECHECK_FAILED_STORAGE, MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT), metadata (redacted). Redaction: No secrets, tokens, or raw kubeconfig content; we only log references/identifiers and high-level failure categories. Checklists: [x] Emits events for success and failure paths; [x] Handles retries without dupes (dedupe key: upgrade_run_id + stage + step + outcome); [x] Adds unit tests for schema and dedupe; [x] Adds integration test coverage in upgrade test harness; [x] Security review considerations documented in code comments; [x] Docs TODO tracked in ENG-4899 follow-up for external snippets.

title: Create HubSpot client wrapper with retry, rate-limit handling, and token refresh
preview:
## Summary
This PR introduces a shared HubSpot API client wrapper used by the PLG signal router service (ENG-7750). The goal is to make HubSpot writes reliable under bursty load and HubSpot API rate limits, while keeping behavior deterministic and auditable.

Key features:
- OAuth access token refresh (using refresh token) with concurrency-safe caching
- Exponential backoff + jitter for transient errors (429/5xx/network)
- First-class handling for HubSpot rate-limit headers (and Retry-After when present)
- Structured logs + request correlation for audit/debug
- Small adapter layer for common Company operations used by the router (get-by-domain, upsert properties, create engagement note, create task)

## Context / Motivation
During early wiring of the signal router → HubSpot integration, we observed:
- HubSpot returns 429s under modest concurrency even when we are under our own QPS expectations
- retry behavior needs to be consistent across all call sites and avoid stampeding
- token refresh must not create a thundering herd when multiple workers encounter expiration

This wrapper centralizes behavior so follow-up PRs can focus on routing logic rather than HTTP edge cases.

## Design notes
### Retry policy
- Retries enabled for:
  - HTTP 429 (rate limited)
  - HTTP 500/502/503/504
  - connection reset / timeouts / DNS errors
- Retries NOT enabled for:
  - 4xx (other than 429) (treated as caller / data bug)
  - 409 conflicts for create endpoints (caller should use idempotency key or upsert path)

Backoff:
- base = 250ms
- multiplier = 2
- jitter = +/- 30%
- max delay = 10s
- max attempts (including first) = 6

Rate limit handling:
- If `Retry-After` present (seconds), we honor it (bounded by max delay).
- Else we fall back to backoff.
- We also parse HubSpot `x-hubspot-ratelimit-*` headers (when present) and include them in logs for observability.

### Token refresh
- Stores current access token + expiry in memory
- Refresh is guarded by a singleflight lock to prevent parallel refresh storms
- If refresh fails:
  - returns error and logs with `hubspot.auth.refresh_failed=true`
  - does NOT retry indefinitely (caller can decide to retry the outer job)

### Logging / audit
All requests emit structured logs (info on success, warn on retryable failures, error on terminal failure) with:
- `service`, `env`, `hubspot.method`, `hubspot.path`, `hubspot.status`
- `hubspot.request_id` (if HubSpot returns a request id)
- `attempt`, `backoff_ms`, `retry_after_s`
- `ratelimit.daily_remaining`, `ratelimit.per_second_remaining` (when available)
- `correlation_id` (propagated from the router job)

No PII is logged. Request bodies are not logged; we log only a small allowlist of safe identifiers (e.g., HubSpot object id, internal org id) when provided.

## What changed
### New
- `services/signal-router/src/hubspot/client.ts`:
  - thin HTTP wrapper around fetch/axios (currently axios) with retry + refresh
- `services/signal-router/src/hubspot/auth.ts`:
  - token refresh logic (OAuth) + caching
- `services/signal-router/src/hubspot/errors.ts`:
  - normalized error types so callers can branch (e.g., `RateLimitedError`)
- `services/signal-router/src/hubspot/company.ts`:
  - helper methods for common company ops used by routing
- `services/signal-router/src/metrics/hubspot.ts`:
  - counters/timers hooks (wired to existing metrics interface; dashboards in later PR)

### Updated
- `services/signal-router/src/config.ts`:
  - adds HubSpot settings (timeouts, max attempts)
- `services/signal-router/src/index.ts`:
  - wiring for dependency injection of HubSpot client

## Example usage
Call sites now look like:
- `await hubspot.company.upsertProperties({ companyId, properties }, { correlationId })`
- `await hubspot.tasks.create({ companyId, subject, dueAt }, { correlationId })`

## Tests
- Unit tests for:
  - retry/backoff math (bounded, jittered)
  - honors Retry-After
  - does not retry non-retryable 4xx
  - singleflight token refresh (N callers → 1 refresh)
- Added contract-ish tests with mocked HubSpot responses (nock)

## Load testing results (staging)
Goal: validate behavior under rate limits and ensure we don’t melt HubSpot or our own worker queue.

Setup:
- 8 workers, 32 concurrent HubSpot requests max
- replayed a synthetic workload: 2,000 company property upserts + 2,000 timeline notes
- introduced a controlled 429 rate (HubSpot sandbox + artificial limiter)

Results:
- Without retries: ~11–18% write failure (429/5xx)
- With this client:
  - terminal failure rate: 0.3% (mostly sandbox flakiness / long tail timeouts)
  - p95 end-to-end per request (including backoff): 3.1s
  - max observed backoff: 9.8s
  - token refresh: 1 refresh per expiry window; no parallel refresh storms observed

Notes:
- We should still implement application-level queueing/batching in the router (follow-up PR) to reduce burstiness.

## CI notes
- Initial run failed on `lint` due to import ordering in `hubspot/company.ts` (fixed in 2nd push).
- All checks green as of final commit:
  - build (linux) ✅
  - unit tests ✅
  - typecheck ✅
  - lint ✅

## Security / Compliance
- No additional data is sent to HubSpot beyond what existing router will send; this PR focuses on transport reliability.
- Secrets:
  - uses existing secret store path `hubspot/oauth/refresh_token`
  - access tokens remain in-memory only; no tokens in logs
- Reviewed error logging for potential leakage (no request bodies, no headers persisted).

## Follow-ups
- Wire this client into the routing logic PR (tasks/notes/properties) (ENG-7750)
- Add explicit circuit breaker when HubSpot is degraded (SRE suggestion)
- Add dashboards/alerts once metrics are fully integrated (ENG-7824)

---

## Commits
1. hubspot: add auth refresh + token cache
2. hubspot: implement retry wrapper with 429 awareness + jitter
3. hubspot: normalize error types and logging fields
4. tests: add retry + refresh coverage
5. chore: config plumbing + docs

## Changed areas (high level)
- services/signal-router/src/hubspot/*
- services/signal-router/src/config.ts
- services/signal-router/test/*

---

## Review conversation (highlights)
**David Kim (HubSpot admin):** Are we honoring HubSpot’s guidance on Retry-After and are we tracking request ids?

**Ethan Park (author):** Yes—Retry-After is honored when present (bounded). We also log HubSpot request id when returned and include ratelimit headers in structured logs.

**Marcus Lin (Security):** Please confirm no tokens or request payloads are logged. Also confirm we’re not logging full URLs with query params.

**Ethan Park:** Confirmed. We log path template only (no query), do not log headers, and bodies are not logged. Added explicit redaction guard for auth headers.

**Priya Natarajan (Platform):** Can we ensure refresh is singleflight? Otherwise we’ll spike auth when tokens expire.

**Ethan Park:** Added a singleflight lock + test that 20 concurrent callers result in exactly one refresh.

**Isabelle Laurent (Sales Ops):** Please make sure 4xx failures are clearly categorized so Ops can differentiate data issues vs rate limits.

**Ethan Park:** Added `HubSpotErrorKind` enum + logs include `hubspot.error_kind` (rate_limited | auth | invalid_request | server | network).

**Logan Wright (Telemetry/Analytics):** Any metrics hooks? We’ll need counts of retries/429s.

**Ethan Park:** Included counters/timers interface; actual dashboard wiring is in ENG-7824, but this exposes `hubspot_requests_total`, `hubspot_retries_total`, and `hubspot_request_duration_ms`.

Approvals:
- David Kim ✅
- Priya Natarajan ✅
- Marcus Lin ✅ (after redaction guard)
- Logan Wright ✅

## Merge
Merged via squash into `main`.

title: Burst: rollout toggle by region + hardware class (allow-list) with per-tenant enablement + audit trail
preview:
Context: We need a safe GA ramp for Dedicated burst capacity, staged by region + hardware class (ex: us-east/H100 first) and further gated by explicit per-tenant enablement. This PR adds a control-plane enforced rollout allow-list keyed by (region, hardware_class) and ties it into the existing tenant burst config. It also emits audit events for all rollout policy changes, with RBAC restrictions so only privileged operators can modify the allow-list.

Key behavior:
- A tenant can be configured for burst (tenant.burst.enabled=true), but burst will not be admitted unless the fleet rollout allow-list includes the tenant’s region + hardware_class.
- Allow-list is evaluated deterministically in the control plane policy layer and returned as part of the admission precheck metadata so routing can surface consistent denial reason codes.
- Rollout policy changes are auditable (who/when/what changed), with diffs and correlation IDs.

Implementation notes:
- Adds a new BurstRolloutPolicy object stored in control-plane config (same backing store as other policy bundles), with schema:
  - allowed_pairs: list of "<region>/<hardware_class>" strings (ex: "us-east/H100")
  - default_behavior: "deny" (explicit) (we do not support implicit allow)
  - updated_by, updated_at, change_reason (required)
- Adds Admin API endpoints:
  - GET /v1/admin/burst/rollout-policy
  - PUT /v1/admin/burst/rollout-policy (requires reason + etag for optimistic concurrency)
- Adds RBAC: requires role "fleet-ops" or "platform-admin" for PUT.
- Adds audit events:
  - burst.rollout_policy.updated
  - burst.rollout_policy.read (admin only; for evidence trails during security reviews)
- Wires evaluation into burst config validation and admission precheck response:
  - If tenant is enabled but pair not allow-listed => hard deny with reason_code=BURST_ROLLOUT_NOT_ENABLED_FOR_REGION_HW (non-retryable unless config changes)

Rollout / ops:
- Default policy is empty allow-list => burst effectively disabled everywhere unless explicitly enabled.
- Includes a migration that seeds an empty policy object on deploy.
- Adds a runbook note in code comments pointing to the SRE rollback switch (global kill switch remains the top-level safety control).

Testing:
- Unit tests for parsing/normalizing region/hardware pairs and validation (dedupe, case normalization, rejects unknown regions/hw classes)
- Integration tests for per-tenant enabled + allow-list mismatch => deterministic deny + audit emitted on policy update

Risk:
- Misconfiguration could block burst unexpectedly; mitigated via explicit deny reason code surfaced to operators + read endpoint.

Release notes (internal): Adds staged rollout gating for burst capacity by region + hardware class with audited admin controls.
text:
Context: We need a safe GA ramp for Dedicated burst capacity, staged by region + hardware class (ex: us-east/H100 first) and further gated by explicit per-tenant enablement. This PR adds a control-plane enforced rollout allow-list keyed by (region, hardware_class) and ties it into the existing tenant burst config. It also emits audit events for all rollout policy changes, with RBAC restrictions so only privileged operators can modify the allow-list.

Key behavior:
- A tenant can be configured for burst (tenant.burst.enabled=true), but burst will not be admitted unless the fleet rollout allow-list includes the tenant’s region + hardware_class.
- Allow-list is evaluated deterministically in the control plane policy layer and returned as part of the admission precheck metadata so routing can surface consistent denial reason codes.
- Rollout policy changes are auditable (who/when/what changed), with diffs and correlation IDs.

Implementation notes:
- Adds a new BurstRolloutPolicy object stored in control-plane config (same backing store as other policy bundles), with schema:
  - allowed_pairs: list of "<region>/<hardware_class>" strings (ex: "us-east/H100")
  - default_behavior: "deny" (explicit) (we do not support implicit allow)
  - updated_by, updated_at, change_reason (required)
- Adds Admin API endpoints:
  - GET /v1/admin/burst/rollout-policy
  - PUT /v1/admin/burst/rollout-policy (requires reason + etag for optimistic concurrency)
- Adds RBAC: requires role "fleet-ops" or "platform-admin" for PUT.
- Adds audit events:
  - burst.rollout_policy.updated
  - burst.rollout_policy.read (admin only; for evidence trails during security reviews)
- Wires evaluation into burst config validation and admission precheck response:
  - If tenant is enabled but pair not allow-listed => hard deny with reason_code=BURST_ROLLOUT_NOT_ENABLED_FOR_REGION_HW (non-retryable unless config changes)

Rollout / ops:
- Default policy is empty allow-list => burst effectively disabled everywhere unless explicitly enabled.
- Includes a migration that seeds an empty policy object on deploy.
- Adds a runbook note in code comments pointing to the SRE rollback switch (global kill switch remains the top-level safety control).

Testing:
- Unit tests for parsing/normalizing region/hardware pairs and validation (dedupe, case normalization, rejects unknown regions/hw classes)
- Integration tests for per-tenant enabled + allow-list mismatch => deterministic deny + audit emitted on policy update

Risk:
- Misconfiguration could block burst unexpectedly; mitigated via explicit deny reason code surfaced to operators + read endpoint.

Release notes (internal): Adds staged rollout gating for burst capacity by region + hardware class with audited admin controls.

title: GA: enable route experiments by default + tighten validations + release notes
preview:
Summary
This PR flips the GA defaults for route-level experiments (Console Rollouts) and tightens validations across the control plane + policy evaluator, plus wires up release notes + rollout checklist for the 2025.04 train.

Context
During beta we saw two recurring issues:
1) Variant skew when a stable stickiness key was missing or low-entropy (SUP-24831, see also follow-up fixes in pr-28705).
2) Customers accidentally creating configs that were "valid" structurally but unsafe operationally (overlapping experiments, impossible allocations, incompatible model variants, and confusing stop semantics).

This PR is intentionally not adding statistical significance analysis (explicit non-goal for GA per PM-482). It focuses on making the defaults safe and predictable.

What changed (high level)
- GA enablement defaults:
  - Route experiments are enabled by default for eligible orgs behind a staged rollout gate (see "Rollout plan" below).
  - New orgs created after GA gate automatically get the capability (no manual flag required), but actions remain RBAC-gated.
  - Existing orgs are enabled in waves by region/account tier with an immediate rollback lever.

- Deterministic assignment defaults (safe handling of missing identifiers):
  - Stickiness key precedence updated to:
    1) Explicit customer-provided header: x-redwood-sticky-key (if present)
    2) customer_id (authenticated requests)
    3) api_key_id (fallback for service-to-service)
    4) If none are present: assign to "control" variant only (no split) and emit a structured warning event + Console validation warning
  - This prevents low-entropy defaults that caused skew and makes the "missing id" behavior visible.
  - Debug endpoint output updated to show which key path was used (ties into pr-28596) without leaking raw identifiers (redacted).

- Validation hardening (control plane + Console API):
  - Allocation rules:
    - Enforce allocations sum to 1.0 (within epsilon), no negative values, and max N variants (GA default: 5).
    - Enforce minimum non-zero split (GA default: 1%) unless experiment is in "draft".
  - Route overlap rules:
    - Block multiple ACTIVE experiments targeting the same normalized route_id.
    - Allow an ACTIVE + DRAFT overlap for editing, but DRAFT cannot be started until the active one is stopped.
    - Error messages include the conflicting experiment id and a deep link to Console.
  - Variant compatibility:
    - Validate variant model compatibility with route schema (chat vs completions, tool calling enablement, structured output requirements).
    - Validate fallback interactions: experiments apply before fallback evaluation (consistent with pr-28457); config that would "mask" variant selection via fallback is rejected with a remediation hint.
  - Stop semantics:
    - GA default remains soft-stop on guardrail breach (mark degraded + notify). Optional hard-stop remains opt-in and is protected by a kill switch (see below).

- Guardrail defaults (GA):
  - Default thresholds applied on creation (editable):
    - p99 latency regression: +25% vs control (warning), +50% (eligible for auto-stop if hard-stop enabled)
    - error rate: +0.5% absolute vs control (warning)
    - cost/token: +15% vs control (warning)
  - Console copy updated to clarify that guardrails evaluate on aggregated windows and may lag during backfills.

- Release notes + operational readiness:
  - Added 2025.04 release note entry and internal rollout checklist (feature flag, dashboards, runbooks, support macros).
  - Added runbook links in the guardrail banners + alert payload wiring (consistent with pr-28734).

Rollout plan (staged)
- Phase A (Day 0): Enable for internal + existing beta allowlist accounts in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Monitor:
  - config propagation lag p95
  - attribution drop rate
  - split accuracy monitors
  - DB query cost for results endpoint
- Phase B (Day 2-3): Expand to remaining regions for allowlisted accounts.
- Phase C (Week 1): Enable for all "Pro" tier accounts.
- Phase D (Week 2): Enable for all remaining eligible accounts.

Roll-back plan
- Immediate: global kill switch to revert "enabled by default" back to explicit opt-in (keeps APIs functional for already running experiments, but blocks new starts).
- If routing anomalies: force evaluator to route 100% to control for all experiments (separate emergency flag), preserving attribution headers for debugging.
- If metrics pipeline issues: disable high-cardinality expansions; keep minimal dimensions only (see ADR-048 + pr-28741 follow-ups).

Files / areas touched
- control-plane/experiment-service:
  - GA validation rules, improved error messages, and audit payload change-diff summary fields
- policy-engine / router:
  - stickiness key precedence + missing-id behavior; additional metrics for warning rate
- console-api:
  - surface validation errors consistently; enforce RBAC on new defaults (no permission changes, but safer gating)
- docs/release-notes:
  - 2025.04 notes and rollout checklist section

Commit summary
- feat(experiments): default-enable route experiments w/ staged rollout gate
- fix(assignment): safe missing-id behavior (force control) + warning events
- chore(validation): block overlapping ACTIVE experiments + tighten allocation constraints
- feat(console): improve validation error mapping + GA default guardrails
- docs(release): add 2025.04 route experiments GA notes + runbook links

Testing
- Unit: experiment schema validation, overlap rules, stickiness precedence
- Integration: deterministic bucketing stability across restarts, split distribution simulation, stop/start state transitions
- E2E (smoke): create experiment -> start -> observe attribution headers -> stop (drain)

Checklist
- [x] GA defaults approved by Product (Marissa Cole)
- [x] SRE review for rollout + kill switches (Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes)
- [x] Telemetry reviewed for cardinality risk (Logan Wright)
- [x] Security review for debug output redaction + audit fields (Marcus Lin)
- [x] Release notes updated for 2025.04

Notable review discussion (captured here for posterity)
- SRE requested that "missing stickiness key" never silently hashes low-entropy attributes; we now force control and emit a warning event.
- Telemetry requested keeping experiment dimensions minimal by default; this PR does not change the tagging strategy beyond adding a warning counter.
- Product requested clearer Console copy around soft-stop vs hard-stop; updated in the wizard + details page tooltips.

Related PRs / references
- pr-28673 (feature flag gates for beta enablement)
- pr-28692 (config versioning/migrations)
- pr-28705 (skew fix when sticky key missing)
- pr-28741 (metrics cardinality reduction)

CI
- build: passed
- lint: passed
- unit tests: passed
- integration tests: passed
- e2e-smoke: passed (reran once due to transient env timeout)

Merge
Squash merged into main for the 2025.04 release train. Release coordinator: Connor O'Brien.

title: RBAC: add permissions and default roles for SIEM audit export management
preview:
Implements least-privilege RBAC permissions/scopes for the SIEM-ready audit export beta. This PR introduces explicit permissions for (a) managing audit export configurations (create/update/delete/enable/disable), (b) rotating/attaching destination credentials, and (c) viewing exporter delivery health/status (lag, last success, last error) in Console and via API. Permissions are wired through the control-plane authz layer and included in default enterprise roles with feature-flag gating. Linked: ENG-5173.

Motivation: We need to allow Security/Compliance and Workspace Admins to manage export destinations without granting broad platform-admin access, while ensuring auditors/support can view delivery health without being able to exfiltrate secrets or modify destinations.

Scope:
- New permission names + descriptions in the RBAC registry
- Policy checks in the control-plane endpoints for export config CRUD and status
- Default role mappings for "Workspace Admin", "Security Admin", and "Auditor" (beta)
- Explicit denial of secret material access via status endpoints (status returns credential_ref metadata only)

Non-scope:
- Fine-grained per-destination egress allowlist policy (separate PR in exporter)
- UI changes (Console will consume the status endpoint + permission-gated actions)

Rollout:
- Permission checks are enabled regardless, but routes are still gated behind the SIEM export feature flag + tenant allowlist (ENG-5184).

Test plan:
- Added unit tests for permission evaluation and role mapping
- Added request tests for export config endpoints returning 403 for insufficient perms
- Manual: verified Admin can create config; Auditor can view status but cannot edit/rotate creds

Security notes:
- Status endpoint returns only redacted error messages (no headers/tokens) and a credential reference ID, not secret values.
- "Rotate credentials" is its own permission to avoid accidental broad write access.

Docs:
- Internal permission catalog updated (follow-up for external admin docs tracked in PM-858).

title: Admin override for onboarding checklist (Support/CS) + audit logging
preview:
Context: Support/CS needs a safe way to mark checklist steps complete/reset during onboarding troubleshooting (e.g., customer rotated keys, telemetry backfill lag, or a step was missed due to earlier event-ordering issues). This PR adds admin-only override endpoints to the checklist progress service, plus audit logging and minimal UI plumbing for internal tools (Console will consume in a follow-up PR).

What’s included: (1) Admin override API endpoints to mark a checklist step COMPLETE/NOT_STARTED (or clear an override) for a tenant, with optional annotation text and an internal reason code; (2) Strict authorization (RBAC) so only Support/CS roles can call the endpoints, scoped to internal operators; (3) Immutable audit log entries capturing actor, tenant, step, previous state, new state, reason, and timestamp; (4) Telemetry events for override actions (PII-free) so we can monitor frequency and identify systemic onboarding issues; (5) Guardrails: overrides are additive on top of event-derived state and never delete raw events; step state transitions remain monotonic unless an override explicitly resets.

Why: Dogfood surfaced that a small number of tenants get “stuck” when event emission is delayed or the customer changes org structure. Support currently uses ad-hoc SQL/scripts to unblock customers; this creates risk and lacks auditability. We want an approved, logged, least-privilege path.

API summary: POST /internal/admin/onboarding/checklist/tenants/{tenant_id}/steps/{step_id}:override with body {"action": "MARK_COMPLETE"|"RESET"|"CLEAR_OVERRIDE", "reason_code": "AUTH_ROTATION"|"TELEMETRY_DELAY"|"CUSTOMER_CONFIRMED"|"OTHER", "note": "freeform"}. GET /internal/admin/onboarding/checklist/tenants/{tenant_id}/audit returns latest override actions (paginated).

Permissions: New RBAC permission "support:checklist_override" (role-bound to Support + CS internal roles). Requests require an operator identity (no API keys) and are denied for customer principals.

Audit retention: Audit records follow existing admin-action retention (18 months) and are excluded from customer-accessible logs. Notes are limited to 500 chars and should not contain customer secrets/PII.

Testing: Unit tests for authorization, idempotency, and audit writes; integration tests for typical override flows; added data-contract test to ensure override telemetry payload stays within cardinality constraints.

Follow-ups: (a) Console UI admin drawer + Support macro deep links (tracked in ENG-5852 subtask); (b) Add a “reason required” enforcement in UI for RESET actions; (c) Consider restricting RESET to a smaller set of steps if we see misuse.

title: Control plane: audit export config CRUD endpoints + validation (feature-flagged)
preview: Implements the first-cut control-plane API surface for SIEM-ready audit export config management, gated behind the `siem_audit_export_beta` feature flag. This PR adds CRUD endpoints for export configurations (S3 / Splunk HEC / Datadog Logs), request validation (destination-specific, filter schema, and backfill window constraints), and an audit trail for config changes (create/update/disable/delete + credential rotation reference updates). The API is intended to be consumed by Console (settings page) and by the exporter service config sync. Notes: filtering evaluation stays in the exporter for now, but we validate filter shape + safe defaults here to prevent pathological configs. RBAC integration is stubbed to existing admin checks in this PR; fine-grained permissions land in follow-up (ENG-5173 / PR-28792). Endpoints: `GET /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/audit-exports/configs` (list), `POST /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/audit-exports/configs` (create), `GET /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/audit-exports/configs/{config_id}` (get), `PATCH /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/audit-exports/configs/{config_id}` (update/disable), `DELETE /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/audit-exports/configs/{config_id}` (delete soft-delete). Includes `POST /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/audit-exports/configs/{config_id}:test` for destination connectivity smoke checks (no event payloads; uses destination adapter preflight in a controlled sandbox). Storage: new `audit_export_configs` table + migrations; config payload stored as typed columns + JSONB for destination settings with strict schema. Audit events: emits `audit.export_config.*` actions with stable `event_id`, actor, workspace, config_id, destination_type, and minimal redacted settings (no secrets). Feature flag behavior: when disabled, endpoints return 404 to avoid API surface discovery; when enabled but tenant not allowlisted (handled in follow-up), behavior is controlled by the existing enterprise beta gate (temporary). Backfill semantics: create supports an optional `backfill_start` up to 7 days in the past; update allows moving forward only (no widening).

title: RBAC v2: add end-to-end integration tests for critical permissions (keys, roles, routing policies)
preview: Context: As part of RBAC v2 rollout we’ve been relying on unit tests + endpoint annotation coverage, but we’ve still had a few regressions where critical routes either (a) were reachable through alternate gateway paths (streaming vs non-streaming), or (b) had inconsistent enforcement between gateway and control-plane services. This PR adds end-to-end integration tests that exercise the full request path (gateway -> service) and validates allow/deny decisions for a small set of high-risk actions: API key rotation, role updates/bindings, and routing policy edits. What’s included: (1) New test harness utilities to create org/project/env fixtures and issue signed test tokens for users with specific RBAC v2 role bindings. (2) E2E tests for API key rotation endpoints: verifies that users without `keys.rotate` (or equivalent) receive 403 with structured denial code; verifies that a user with the permission can rotate and that the rotated secret is unusable/usable as expected. (3) E2E tests for role management: verifies deny-by-default on role creation/update/binding for a non-admin; verifies that Security Admin can update bindings; verifies that a user cannot grant permissions they don’t hold when `rbacv2_prevent_privilege_escalation` is enabled. (4) E2E tests for routing policy edits: verifies that Deployment Operator can update routing policy within the scoped environment, and that the same user is denied when attempting cross-environment edits; validates audit event fields include `authz.decision`, `authz.policy_version`, and `authz.denial_reason_code` when denied. (5) Runs the suite in CI as part of the existing integration pipeline (tagged `rbacv2_critical`). Notes: tests are feature-flag aware and run in dual-read mode; they assert behavior under `RBAC_V2_ENFORCE=true` and fail fast if the gateway is misconfigured (no authz middleware). text: Context: As part of RBAC v2 rollout we’ve been relying on unit tests + endpoint annotation coverage, but we’ve still had a few regressions where critical routes either (a) were reachable through alternate gateway paths (streaming vs non-streaming), or (b) had inconsistent enforcement between gateway and control-plane services. This PR adds end-to-end integration tests that exercise the full request path (gateway -> service) and validates allow/deny decisions for a small set of high-risk actions: API key rotation, role updates/bindings, and routing policy edits. What’s included: (1) New test harness utilities to create org/project/env fixtures and issue signed test tokens for users with specific RBAC v2 role bindings. (2) E2E tests for API key rotation endpoints: verifies that users without `keys.rotate` (or equivalent) receive 403 with structured denial code; verifies that a user with the permission can rotate and that the rotated secret is unusable/usable as expected.
