(Unredacted diff remains an internal-only admin workflow outside of Slack/incident-bot.) - Backwards compat: `GET /v1/config/diff` behavior remains but now enforces policy; callers without the new scope/role will receive `403` with a remediation hint.

Changed areas (high level): - services/config/api/config_diff_handler.go: enforce scopes/roles, add break-glass handler and TTL grant cache. - services/config/authz/policy_rules.rego: new rules for config diff read and break-glass paths. - services/config/audit/audit_events.go: new event emitters + field normalization. - services/config/limits/rate_limits.yaml: per-endpoint budgets for diff and break-glass. - tools/policy-bundle-lint/: updated tests and fixtures.

Commits (squash summary): - Add policy rules + unit tests for config diff read permissions - Add break-glass grant cache w/ TTL and rate limits - Emit audit events for diff access + break-glass grants - Wire new OAuth scopes into service auth middleware - Docs: add operational notes for on-call + security review checklist

Testing / validation: - Unit: config diff handler authz matrix tests (service vs user vs break-glass), redaction invariants, TTL expiry. - Integration: policy bundle evaluation tests in CI. - Manual: verified incident-bot service identity can fetch redacted diffs in staging with new scope; verified non-oncall user receives 403; verified Sev2 with break-glass request denied.

Rollout plan: - Land behind config flag `configdiff.enforce_scopes=true` default false in prod for 24h. - Coordinate with incident-bot scope rollout (ENG-5426) and Console role assignment. - After scopes are deployed, flip to true globally. - Monitor: audit event volume, 403 rates on `/v1/config/diff`, and p95 latency.

Reviewer notes / discussion highlights: - Marcus Lin: requested explicit guarantee that break-glass does not enable unredacted output and that severity gating is deny-by-default. Addressed by keeping shared redaction pipeline and adding strict enum parsing with hard fail. - Kevin Osei: asked for tenant id hashing in audit payload and consistent field names. Updated audit emitter to `tenant_id_hash` and aligned with SIEM pipeline expectations. - Rafael Mendes: asked for on-call usability: clear error messaging for responders + TTL visibility. Added `breakglass_grant_expires_at` and standardized 403 remediation hints (“request break-glass for SEV0/SEV1” + internal runbook link).

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - incident-bot: consume `breakglass_grant_expires_at` and display a warning banner in Slack modal (tracked in ENG-5426). - docs/runbooks: add break-glass SOP + escalation path for Sev0/Sev1 approval (ENG-5431). - security: quarterly access review automation for on-call groups (separate ticket).

Screenshots: N/A (API/policy change only).

Checklist: - [x] Added tests for authz matrix - [x] Added/updated audit events - [x] Verified no unredacted diff path introduced - [x] Added rate limits and deny-by-default severity validation - [x] Coordinated scope name with incident-bot + platform teams

title: Control plane: API endpoints for route-level quantization settings (CRUD + RBAC + audit log)
preview:
Motivation
We need a control-plane API surface to store and manage route-level quantization settings for the Quantization Profile Library (v1). This PR adds the first pass at CRUD endpoints, enforces RBAC, and emits audit records so changes are attributable and reversible.

Scope
- Adds RouteQuantizationSettings resource and persistence (backed by existing route_config storage).
- API endpoints:
  - GET /v1/routes/{route_id}/quantization-settings
  - PUT /v1/routes/{route_id}/quantization-settings (upsert)
  - DELETE /v1/routes/{route_id}/quantization-settings (reset to default)
  - GET /v1/orgs/{org_id}/routes/quantization-settings (bulk list for Console)
- RBAC: restricted to org_owner, org_admin, and route_admin roles (same authorization model used for route config edits).
- Audit logging: emits an audit record on create/update/delete with actor, org_id, route_id, and diff summary.
- Validation: basic schema validation + guardrails:
  - quantization_profile_ref is either "default" or a versioned profile ref string (e.g. "qpl:llama-3.1-70b-instruct:int8:v3").
  - allow_preview flag is accepted but does not change serving behavior; it’s used by Console for UX preview flows.
  - if global kill-switch is active, PUT returns 409 with a clear message (so Console can surface it).

Out of scope (handled in follow-ups)
- Compatibility evaluation against model/hardware/ctx length (policy engine will enforce deterministic apply vs fallback).
- Console UI wiring (handled by Console backend + frontend PRs).
- Dedicated/Private propagation details (this PR only covers the hosted control-plane API contract).

Implementation notes
- Uses optimistic concurrency on PUT via If-Match with route_config revision; returns 412 on conflict.
- All endpoints return a canonical Settings payload with resolved defaults (i.e., DELETE returns disabled/default state).
- Bulk list endpoint is paginated and supports route_id filtering for Console.

Testing
- Unit tests: authz matrix, request validation, ETag conflict behavior, audit write behavior.
- Integration test (light): round-trip create->get->update->delete in a local control-plane env.

Rollout
- Endpoints are behind feature flag: quant_profiles_route_settings_api (default off).
- No migrations required; stored as a new typed subdocument in route_config with backward-compatible serialization.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-5814
- Confluence: Quantization profile config service (control-plane)
- Related PRs: pr-18411 (route config field), pr-18427 (policy compatibility guardrails), pr-18488 (kill-switch)

Screenshots/Examples
Example PUT payload:
{
  "quantization_profile_ref": "qpl:llama-3.1-70b-instruct:int8:v3",
  "mode": "enabled"
}
Example response includes:
- mode: enabled|disabled
- quantization_profile_ref: default|<versioned-ref>
- updated_by, updated_at

Risk
Moderate. API-only change behind a feature flag; serving behavior remains unchanged unless downstream components read/act on these settings (gated separately).

title: Redact secrets in config diffs before rendering to Slack
preview: Problem: Incident-bot v2 is starting to surface tenant-safe config diffs in #incidents and #eng-oncall, but the current formatting is purely syntactic and can leak secrets if upstream config contains inline credentials/tokens (or if a value looks like a secret). This PR adds a deterministic redaction layer that runs before any diff content is rendered to Slack (cards, modals, and copy-to-clipboard). Approach: (1) Add key-based redaction (denylist of sensitive key names + path-based matches). (2) Add value-based secret detection (JWTs, API keys, common token prefixes, PEM blocks, base64-ish long strings, AWS-like access keys, etc.) with conservative thresholds. (3) Add allowlisted keys/paths that are safe to show even if they trip generic heuristics (e.g., numeric limits, routing weights, non-sensitive feature flag booleans). (4) Replace redacted values with stable placeholders ("[REDACTED:key]" / "[REDACTED:pattern]"), preserving diff structure for incident triage. (5) Add tests ensuring we never render raw secret patterns to Slack output (unit tests + golden snapshots for Slack blocks). Also adds a visible warning banner when redaction occurs and a counter metric to help tune false positives over time. Notes: This PR does not change authorization; it is a defense-in-depth layer. It intentionally over-redacts in ambiguous cases. Follow-up: align patterns with redwood/config-service redaction hooks once that endpoint ships (ENG-5422) and wire audit events for "redaction_applied" vs "no_redaction" for easier compliance review.

title: Improve rate limits + circuit breakers for diagnostics endpoints (incident-bot/Console)
preview: Context: During incidents we see a spike in diagnostics traffic driven by incident-bot v2 thread interactions + Console deep links. When upstream telemetry/config systems are degraded, retries/timeouts can turn into a burst that (a) increases tail latencies for responders and (b) adds load to already-sick dependencies. This PR adds explicit rate limits and circuit breakers to the diagnostics surface area (telemetry aggregation, config diff, and deploy metadata) to prevent self-inflicted load while still keeping the bot/Console useful in degraded conditions. Changes: (1) Introduce a shared "Diagnostics Guard" middleware that applies token-bucket limits (per service identity, per endpoint, and per tenant when available) with a small global safety cap; returns bounded responses with retry-after hints. (2) Add a dependency-aware circuit breaker around outbound calls to telemetry-query, config-service diff, and deploy-registry. Breaker opens on sustained p95 latency + elevated error rate; while open, endpoints return cached context (if present) plus deep links and a "degraded mode" banner instead of attempting live fanout. (3) Implement request coalescing + singleflight for identical diagnostic queries within a short window (reduces thundering herd from Slack retries). (4) Add load-shedding behavior: when diagnostics budget is exhausted, we skip expensive dimensions (tenant breakdown, high-cardinality labels) and fall back to region/service aggregates. (5) Metrics + alerts: export diagnostics_budget_dropped_requests_total, diagnostics_cb_state, diagnostics_upstream_latency_ms, and cache_hit_ratio. Wire to existing incident context dashboards to make the new behavior visible. (6) Configuration: add defaults tuned for #incidents usage, plus an allowlist override for SRE break-glass service identity (still audited; does not bypass the global cap). Testing: unit tests for token bucket math, breaker transitions, and degraded-mode payloads; integration tests with a fake upstream that delays/fails; manual validation by replaying a captured incident-bot interaction pattern from INT-3341. Rollout: behind a runtime flag DIAGNOSTICS_GUARD_ENABLED (default ON in staging, OFF in prod). After merge we will enable in prod for incident-bot service identity first, then Console server over 48h if no regression in diagnostics latency. Notes: This intentionally does not change Slack bot retry behavior (handled in incident-bot repo) but makes the backend resilient to those retries. Related: PR-29125 (tenant-aware metric query primitives), PR-29133 (audit events for bot access), ENG-5427 (telemetry caching/coalescing), ENG-5429 (bot load shedding).

title: Add time-windowed exports + actor/event-type filters (report templates)
preview:
## Summary
This PR enhances `audit-log-exporter` to support compliance/evidence workflows by adding (1) explicit time-windowed exports and (2) optional filters for actor + event types. It also introduces a small report-template layer so the compliance reporting pack can request a known preset ("last_90_days_admin_activity", "rbac_changes", etc.) without re-encoding filter logic in multiple places.

Motivation: enterprise security reviews are consistently asking for "audit logs for a defined period" and "only admin/security-relevant events". We also had a support report (SUP-3917) where customers believed exports were missing event types; part of that was unclear defaults and lack of an explicit event-type filter list in the export request.

## API changes
### New request fields (non-breaking)
- `start_time` (RFC3339, inclusive)
- `end_time` (RFC3339, exclusive)
- `actor_ids` (optional array of strings)
- `actor_types` (optional array: `user|service_account|api_key`)
- `event_types` (optional array of strings; validated against known types)
- `template_id` (optional string; mutually exclusive with providing filters directly unless `allow_override=true`)

Behavior:
- If `start_time`/`end_time` are omitted, behavior remains "export all available within retention" (existing behavior).
- If provided, exporter enforces the window at query time and re-checks in stream/iteration to avoid boundary drift.
- `event_types` is an allowlist filter; unknown types return 400 with a list of valid types.
- `actor_ids` and `actor_types` are ANDed with the time-window and event filter.

### Report templates
Adds `templates/*.yaml` defining a stable set of presets that map to filters + default time windows. Example preset:
- `rbac_changes_last_180d` -> `event_types=[rbac.role.created, rbac.role.updated, rbac.binding.created, rbac.binding.deleted]`, window=180d

Template contract:
- Templates are versioned by exporter release; template IDs are stable.
- Export response includes `applied_filters` so customers/auditors can see exactly what was used.

## Implementation notes
- Query layer updated to accept a `FilterSpec` that compiles to SQL WHERE clauses + parameter binding.
- Streaming export code now emits a header block with: `export_id`, `generated_at`, `window`, `filters`, `template_id` (if any).
- Added a canonical "known event types" registry and tests ensuring registry coverage matches the producer schema we expect.
- Pagination: time-windowed exports use `(timestamp, id)` cursor to avoid duplicates when multiple events share the same timestamp.

## Security/Compliance considerations
- Filters are validated server-side (no raw SQL fragments; strict param binding).
- `actor_ids` is limited to 200 entries to cap query size.
- Maximum export window defaults to 365 days unless `--allow-large-windows` is enabled (intended for internal GRC workflows only).
- Added explicit audit event for `audit_log.export.generated` that records: caller identity, window, filters, and bytes written.

## Testing
- Unit tests: filter compilation, RFC3339 parsing, template resolution, unknown event-type validation.
- Integration tests: seeded audit events across boundary timestamps; verifies inclusive/exclusive semantics.
- Load sanity: ran export for 90d in staging with 1.2M rows; query time stable and memory constant in streaming mode.

## Rollout
- Default behavior unchanged. Consumers can adopt templates incrementally.
- Follow-up: wire `template_id` usage into compliance report pack generator (ENG-18441) once this merges.

## Example request
`POST /v1/exports`
{
  "template_id": "rbac_changes_last_180d",
  "start_time": "2024-08-01T00:00:00Z",
  "end_time": "2025-02-01T00:00:00Z"
}

## Checklist
- [x] Backwards compatible
- [x] Tests added
- [x] Docs updated (README + examples)
- [x] Security review notes captured (filters + limits)
- [x] Added exporter-side audit event for export generation

title: Add internal feature flag + RBAC gating for Unit Economics dashboard
preview: Context: The new internal Unit Economics dashboard surfaces sensitive cost data (blended $/1M output tokens, drilldowns by model/region/tier). We need to ensure access is restricted to a small internal allowlist initially and that all access is auditable for Finance and Security sign-off. This PR adds (1) a server-side feature flag gate and (2) RBAC policy updates + enforcement in the Console backend so that the Unit Economics endpoints and UI route are not reachable unless explicitly granted. Changes: - Feature flag: add `console.internal_unit_economics_dashboard` (server-side, env-scoped). Default OFF in all environments; can be enabled in prod only for the internal allowlist group. - RBAC: introduce permission `console:unit_economics:read` and require it for all unit economics API endpoints + the console route loader. - Enforcement points: apply gating at the HTTP router layer and again at the service layer (defense-in-depth) so future endpoints don’t accidentally bypass checks. - Audit logging: emit an audit event on successful access and on denied access (includes actor, org, reason: missing_flag vs missing_permission). - Admin tooling: add a small helper to map the initial allowlist group to the permission for dogfood rollout. Notes: - The UI still renders behind the flag, but if the backend gate denies access, the page shows the standard “Permission denied” state (UI work in PR-19378). - This intentionally does not add any customer-level permissions; v1 is internal-only (no customer exposure). Testing: unit tests cover flag off -> 404/403 behavior, permission missing -> 403, both enabled -> success, and audit event emission. Follow-ups: - Once Finance signs off on metric definitions, we can expand the allowlist and/or move to role-based groups (Finance, Platform Eng, SRE) instead of named individuals. - Consider adding a “redacted mode” for broader internal visibility if needed (out of v1 scope).

title: Control Plane: annotate sensitive endpoints with RBAC v2 permissions + coverage tests
preview: Context: As part of RBAC v2, we need a canonical mapping from Control Plane API actions to permission strings and a mechanical way to ensure new endpoints can’t ship without being categorized. This PR adds explicit permission annotations to the most security-relevant Control Plane routes (org/user/role management and routing policy updates) and introduces unit tests that fail if a sensitive endpoint is missing a permission. This is intended to support deny-by-default enforcement in the gateway/service middleware (separate PRs), and to improve auditability by guaranteeing an action string exists for every sensitive operation. Scope: (1) Add @RequiresPermission(...) annotations for org/user/role CRUD, API key admin surfaces that live in the Control Plane, and routing policy create/update/delete + rollout/pinning operations. (2) Introduce an endpoint coverage test that enumerates registered routes and asserts that any route tagged as "sensitive" is annotated with a permission and resource scope mapping. (3) Normalize a handful of legacy endpoints to use consistent "resource" extraction (org_id/project_id/env_id) so the evaluator can be called with the correct scope. Non-goals: This PR does not enable enforcement by itself; enforcement remains behind rbac.v2.enforce_control_plane and rbac.v2.enforce_gateway. It also does not finalize role templates or migration mappings; those are handled in the policy bundle repo. Implementation notes: Permission strings follow ADR-014 conventions (e.g., org.users.create, org.roles.update, routing.policies.update, routing.rollouts.manage). title: Backpressure controls + max lag safety (auto-pause) for downstream outages
preview:
Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit backpressure controls and a max-lag safety mechanism to keep the system stable during downstream outages while preserving at-least-once semantics.

What’s in this PR: (1) Max lag threshold per destination with auto-pause: if export_lag_seconds for a (tenant, destination) exceeds configurable max_lag_seconds for a sustained window, we stop pulling new batches for that destination and mark it PAUSED (reason=MAX_LAG) while continuing to process other destinations/tenants. (2) Backpressure based on in-flight bytes and retry backlog: introduce per-destination in_flight_bytes limits and a bounded retry queue; when exceeded we apply pull-side backoff rather than pushing more work into retries. (3) Circuit breaker integration for adapters: adapter-specific throttling signals (e.g., Splunk 429, Datadog 429/5xx) now feed a shared circuit breaker that increases backoff, and can trip into PAUSED when combined with lag growth. (4) Operator controls: new CLI commands to unpause a destination and to override max lag for controlled recovery. (5) Observability: new Prometheus metrics and structured log fields for pause/unpause transitions, reasons, and “time paused”; status endpoint now includes pause state and last_pause_reason.

Delivery semantics: No events are dropped. Auto-pause stops the exporter from advancing its checkpoint for the paused destination, so events remain available for replay/backfill once the downstream recovers and the destination is unpaused. This is consistent with our at-least-once contract; downstream dedup remains based on event_id.

Configuration: Adds exporter config keys (with safe defaults): max_lag_seconds (default 21600 / 6h), max_lag_grace_period_seconds (default 300), max_in_flight_bytes (default 64MiB), max_retry_queue_batches (default 500), pause_cooldown_seconds (default 900). All are feature-flagged behind BACKPRESSURE_V1 to limit beta blast radius.

Testing: unit tests for PauseController transitions; integration test with Splunk stub that returns sustained 429 and verifies: lag rises -> pause triggers -> pulling stops -> unpause resumes and drains; chaos-ish test ensures healthy destination continues despite one paused. Load test notes included in commit message, based on SRE backpressure experiments doc.

Rollout plan: Enable BACKPRESSURE_V1 for beta tenants only after deploying exporter + dashboards. Default max lag is conservative; SRE can tune per-tenant via config overrides if needed.

Checklist: [x] Metrics + dashboards hooks (exporter_paused, pause_reason, pause_duration_seconds) [x] Runbook updates TODO in separate PR (Sean requested) [x] Security review: no new PII; logs redact destination secrets [x] Feature flag gated (BACKPRESSURE_V1) [x] Integration tests cover 429/5xx.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) surface pause/unpause controls in Console (control plane) (b) alerting thresholds tuning once we have 1-2 weeks of beta data (c) doc updates for customer-facing explanation of auto-pause + recovery expectations.

title: Add config diff collector (config-service integration) + Slack formatting
preview:
Summary
This PR wires Incident Bot v2 into the new config-service diff endpoint and adds a first-pass Slack-safe rendering for config diffs. The goal is to surface tenant-safe config deltas (feature flags, routing policy, quotas/rate limits, model pins) in the first 5 minutes of an incident without responders needing to jump through manual queries.

Scope
- Adds a ConfigDiffCollector that calls config-service (internal) to fetch a bounded diff for a (tenant, service, region, time window).
- Adds basic formatting for Slack: compact summary card + optional “expand” view payload (used by later Slack modal work).
- Adds guardrails: query timeouts, size limits, allowlisted keys (with explicit “redacted/omitted” markers), and stable ordering to reduce diff noise.
- Adds audit context headers (requestor + incident thread id) so config-service can emit audit events (event emission handled in redwood PRs; this bot PR only propagates IDs).

Why
We’ve had multiple incidents where 503s/latency spikes correlated with a config push (feature flag, routing policy, rate limits) but on-call had no fast way to see what changed. This PR is the plumbing so the bot can attach “what changed recently” in a customer/tenant-safe way. This directly supports ENG-5422 and the broader “Incident bot + diagnostics improvements” initiative.

Design notes
- Endpoint: config-service Diff API (tenant-aware). We send: tenant_id (or “platform” for global), service, region, env, time_range (default: last 2h), and an allowlist profile.
- Redaction strategy: this PR implements a bot-side allowlist and masking of obviously-sensitive values (tokens/keys), but the source-of-truth redaction is expected to be enforced by config-service. Bot rendering treats anything not explicitly allowlisted as omitted and does not attempt to “best effort” show unknown fields.
- Bounds:
  - Timeout: 2.5s budget (collector) with a single retry at 200ms jittered backoff when error is network/5xx.
  - Diff size: max 50 changes rendered inline; remainder summarized as “+N more changes (open Console link)”.
  - Value length: truncates at 120 chars with suffix “…” and preserves before/after hashes when truncation occurs.
- Degraded mode: on timeout or permission failure, the card renders a short error + deep link stub (Console URL placeholder; final Console routing is handled in later PRs).

User-facing behavior (Slack)
- New “Config changes” section in the incident context payload (not yet enabled by default; gated behind a feature flag checked by the bot runtime).
- Inline diff uses a consistent, minimal format:
  - Added: "+ key = value"
  - Removed: "- key"
  - Modified: "~ key: old -> new"
  - Metadata line: tenant/service/region + time window + "source: config-service"
- If the diff contains any omitted/redacted fields, we show a warning line: "Some fields were omitted for safety. Request elevated access if needed." (copy: per DES-205 guidance, but final wording can change).

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - allowlist filtering and stable ordering
  - truncation rules
  - timeout + retry behavior (fake client)
  - Slack rendering snapshot for representative diffs
- Manual test:
  - ran bot locally against staging config-service with a seeded config change; verified no secrets rendered and ordering remains stable.

Rollout
- Feature flagged behind incident_bot_v2_config_diff (default OFF).
- Intended initial cohort: SRE rotation only in #incidents (enforced via existing cohort gate; broader rollout in ENG-5428).

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Stronger redaction patterns + tests (tracked in PR-1857).
- Slack modal pagination for large diffs (tracked in PR-1854).
- Console deep link finalization + correct incident time windows (Product/Console work).

Checklist
- [x] Bound query cost (timeout + size limits)
- [x] No tenant/customer sensitive fields printed outside allowlist
- [x] Added tests for formatting + bounds
- [x] Documented failure modes in code comments

Review notes / discussion (highlights)
- Kaitlyn Nguyen: “Please ensure we don’t ever show unknown keys by accident. Allowlist should be default-deny.”
  - Devon Price: “Done. Renderer only sees allowlisted keys; everything else is summarized as omitted. Added a test that unknown keys never reach Slack payload.”
- Benji Okafor: “Can we make the output stable so diffs don’t flip-flop between runs?”
  - Devon Price: “Sorted by key path + change type. Added unit test to assert ordering.”
- Marcus Lin: “Bot should not invent redaction; rely on config-service for authoritative redaction. Bot can add ‘safety belt’ masking, but don’t treat it as compliance.”
  - Devon Price: “Agreed. Updated comments and made masking minimal (only obvious secret patterns). We still default-deny on keys.”
- Jacob Stein: “2.5s seems high for incidents when Slack actions have ack deadlines.”
  - Devon Price: “Collector runs asynchronously relative to Slack ack; still, reduced from 4s to 2.5s and added a ‘fallback to link’ error message. We’ll tune with telemetry once query cache lands.”

CI
- ✅ build
- ✅ lint
- ✅ unit
- ✅ integration: incident-bot payload schema validation

Notes
This PR is intentionally conservative on formatting; it’s meant to unblock attaching config deltas quickly while we iterate on UX and redaction hardening in follow-up PRs.

title: Generate built-in role templates from YAML + linting + versioned release artifacts
preview: Context: As part of RBAC v2 we want built-in role templates (Org Admin, Security Admin, Read-only Auditor, Deployment Operator, etc.) to be (a) human-reviewable, (b) lintable in CI, and (c) published as versioned artifacts that services (Control Plane, Console, Private installer) can consume deterministically. This PR introduces a YAML-first source of truth under policy_bundle/roles/*.yaml and a generator that produces the canonical JSON bundle consumed by platform components. It also adds a lint/validation suite to enforce naming/versioning conventions, uniqueness constraints, and cross-scope safety checks, and formalizes an artifact versioning scheme tied to the repo release version. Summary of changes: (1) New role template YAML schema (name, description, scopes, included_permissions, excluded_permissions, constraints) with examples for common templates; (2) Generator CLI (scripts/generate_role_templates) that outputs dist/role_templates.json and dist/role_templates.min.json with stable ordering and content hashes; (3) Lint rules: permission string format + deprecation markers, role name uniqueness, duplicate permissions, forbidden permission combos for auditor roles, and scope validation (org/project/environment/cluster); (4) Release artifact scheme: dist/ artifacts include a bundle_version (semver) and policy_bundle_revision (git SHA) and we tag releases using vX.Y.Z; consumers pin either semver tag or SHA for deterministic rollouts; (5) CI updates: add "rbac-policy-lint" and "rbac-policy-generate" checks; block merge if generated output is out of date; (6) Docs: CONTRIBUTING notes for adding/modifying templates and guidance on bumping bundle versions. Follow-ups: We will add Private-specific operator roles in pr-201 and wire consumption into redwood monorepo (Control Plane + Console) behind RBAC v2 feature flags.

title: Audit event schema v2: add event_id + category taxonomy (compat mapping)
preview:
Context: As part of SIEM-ready audit export beta (ENG-5127), downstream delivery is at-least-once and we need stable dedup keys plus a consistent category taxonomy for filtering + mapping into Splunk/Datadog. This PR updates core audit event emission to include: (1) `event_id` (stable unique id per event) (2) `schema_version: 2` and canonical `category` / `subcategory` / `action` fields (3) a compatibility mapping layer so existing producers that still emit legacy `event_type`/`operation` continue to work while we progressively migrate call sites.

Summary of changes: Add `event_id` generated at emission time using UUIDv7 (time-ordered, collision-safe) and plumbed through the AuditEvent struct. Introduce an enum-backed category taxonomy with a small canonical set and a `category_version` constant to make future taxonomy evolution explicit. Add a `legacy_event_type` field (optional) and a mapping table from legacy strings to v2 taxonomy, used for any call site still emitting legacy types. Update JSON serialization + redaction hooks so `event_id` is always present and never redacted, while category fields are required and validated. Add golden fixtures + unit tests to lock schema output. No changes to retention/storage semantics in this PR.

Taxonomy (v2, initial): categories = {authn, authz, workspace, api_key, model, deployment, billing, data_access, security, system}. `subcategory` is optional but recommended for high-volume categories (e.g., `api_key.rotation`, `authz.role`, `deployment.route`). `action` remains a verb phrase in present tense (create/update/delete/rotate/enable/disable/login/logout/impersonate/etc) and is required.

Compatibility: Existing events that provide `event_type` will have `legacy_event_type` set, and the mapping table populates `category/subcategory/action`. If mapping is missing, we default to `category=system`, `subcategory=audit.unknown_legacy_type`, `action=emit` and include a structured warning log (rate-limited) so we can clean up unmapped call sites before GA.

Follow-ups: (a) migrate remaining emitters to pass v2 taxonomy explicitly, removing reliance on legacy mapping (b) update docs + examples in security/compliance Confluence (c) exporter service uses `event_id` for idempotency keys (separate PR).

Testing: unit tests for UUIDv7 generation + monotonicity (best-effort), mapping table tests (known legacy types), JSON golden files for representative events, and a redaction regression test to ensure `event_id/category/action` survive redaction.

Rollout: behind `AUDIT_SCHEMA_V2_ENABLED` (default ON in dev/staging, OFF in prod until exporter beta gate is ready). When flag is OFF, we still emit legacy fields only and omit v2 additions.

Risk: Low-medium. Primary risk is unexpected unmapped legacy event types causing `system/audit.unknown_legacy_type` categorization; mitigated via logs + metrics + follow-up cleanup.

title: config-service: add tenant-aware diff endpoint w/ authz + redaction hooks
preview:
Summary
Adds a new internal config diff endpoint to config-service for Incident Bot v2 and Console diagnostics.

Motivation
During incidents we need a fast, tenant-safe way to answer: “what changed in config recently?” without SSHing into nodes or grepping ad-hoc logs. This PR introduces a first-class, audited-by-design diff path that:
- is tenant-aware (explicit tenant context; no implicit globals)
- enforces least-privilege authz (service scopes + on-call break-glass role handled by the auth gateway)
- supports redaction hooks so callers can render safe output in Slack

API
- POST /internal/config/v1/diff
- Request fields: tenant_id (required), environment (required), scope (one of: tenant|region|service), target (e.g. region=eu-west-1, service=hosted-api), from_version (optional), to_version (optional), from_time/to_time (optional, max window enforced), options (format + limits)

title: Emit audit log events for incident-bot access to config diffs + privileged telemetry
preview:
Summary: This PR emits first-class audit log events whenever the incident-bot (and other diagnostics tooling using the same service identity) accesses (a) tenant-aware config diffs and (b) privileged telemetry query endpoints. Events are emitted for both allow and deny outcomes and include enough context for compliance review + incident reconstruction without leaking tenant/customer data. Motivation: Incident Bot v2 adds new context cards (config diffs + saturation signals). These paths cross strict tenant-data handling boundaries; we need auditable access with consistent semantics across hosted/dedicated/private. This also addresses INT-3312 where on-call asked “did the bot actually hit config diff?” and we had no centralized audit trail. What’s included: (1) New audit event schema `audit.event.v1` with two event types: `incident_bot.config_diff.read` and `incident_bot.telemetry.query` (plus `*.denied`). (2) Emission points in `config-service` diff handler and `telemetry-query-service` privileged query handler. (3) Plumbing to send events to the existing audit pipeline (Kafka topic `audit-events-v1` -> SIEM sink) with bounded payload size and strict field allowlisting. (4) Unit + integration tests ensuring we never log raw diff bodies, secrets, raw query strings, or high-cardinality tags. (5) Docs update in internal README describing fields, retention, and how to find events during incident response. Event fields (allowlisted): `event_name`, `event_version`, `timestamp`, `actor_type` (service|user), `actor_id` (service principal), `request_id`, `trace_id` (if present), `incident_id` (if provided by bot), `slack_channel_id` (if provided), `resource_type` (config_diff|telemetry_query), `resource_id` (stable ID, not content), `tenant_ref` (hashed), `environment` (prod|staging), `region`, `scope` (RBAC scope evaluated), `decision` (allow|deny), `deny_reason` (enum), `latency_ms`, `payload_class` (config_diff_summary|telemetry_query_summary), `redaction_applied` (true/false). Explicitly NOT logged: diff content, before/after values, secret-like keys, raw metric query strings, free-form filters, customer identifiers/emails, prompt content. Implementation notes: Config diffs: `GET /internal/config/diff` now emits an audit event after authz evaluation and before response write. `resource_id` is computed as `diff_request_id` (UUID) and includes `config_namespace` and `config_version_from/to` (strings) but never the diff. Tenant is logged as `tenant_ref=sha256(tenant_id + audit_salt)` to support correlation without making tenant IDs broadly searchable in the SIEM. Telemetry: privileged queries (those requiring `telemetry:privileged_read`) emit `incident_bot.telemetry.query` with `query_template_id` (new) rather than the raw query. `query_template_id` is selected from an allowlisted set in code (e.g. `gpu_utilization_p95`, `queue_depth_max`, `kv_cache_pressure_p95`, `admission_control_deny_rate`). If a request attempts a non-allowlisted query under the privileged endpoint we return 400 and emit `*.denied` with `deny_reason=template_not_allowlisted`. Performance/availability: Emission is async best-effort via the existing `AuditEmitter` (buffer + background flush). If the audit pipeline is unavailable, we do not fail the request; we increment `audit_emitter_dropped_total{reason=...}` and attach a warning to logs. This is deliberate to keep incident tooling usable during partial outages, while still surfacing audit pipeline health separately. Testing: Added unit tests to assert: (a) no forbidden keys appear in serialized events, (b) tenant hashing is stable with salt, (c) deny paths emit events, (d) telemetry template selection rejects arbitrary queries. Added an integration test to validate `AuditEmitter` produces to the test Kafka harness and that events are under 4KB. Rollout: This is gated by `AUDIT_EVENTS_ENABLED` (default on in staging, off in prod until toggled by SRE/Sec). Once enabled in prod, we will monitor `audit_emitter_dropped_total` and SIEM ingest lag. Follow-ups: (1) Wire incident-bot request IDs consistently across config + telemetry calls (tracked in ENG-5426). (2) Add dashboards for audit volume and drops (SecOps backlog). (3) Expand event coverage to break-glass flows in PR-29140.

Related: PR-29112 (config diff endpoint), PR-29125 (tenant-aware metric queries), ENG-5426 (permissioning/scopes), Incident Bot v2 design doc.

title: RBAC v2: add feature flags + dual-read evaluation (v1 enforced) for staged rollout
preview:
Context
We need a safe way to roll RBAC v2 into production without breaking existing customers/internal workflows. This PR introduces feature flags and a dual-read evaluation mode where we evaluate both RBAC v1 and RBAC v2 for the same request, continue enforcing v1 (source of truth) during the migration phase, and emit metrics/logs on discrepancies.

What this PR does
- Adds a small set of rollout flags (per-environment) and a consistent config API usable by gateway + control plane:
  - rbac_v2.enabled: enables v2 evaluation codepaths (no enforcement by itself)
  - rbac_v2.dual_read: evaluate v1 + v2 and compare results; enforcement remains v1
  - rbac_v2.enforce: enforce v2 result (guarded; not intended to be turned on broadly yet)
  - rbac_v2.fail_open_on_error (default true in dual-read): if v2 evaluator errors/timeouts, do not block request; record error metrics
  - rbac_v2.sample_rate (default 1.0 in non-prod, 0.1 in prod): controls dual-read comparison sampling to bound overhead
- Adds comparison + observability:
  - Metric: authz.dual_read.total{service,route,action,scope_kind}
  - Metric: authz.dual_read.mismatch{service,route,reason} where reason ∈ {v2_denied_v1_allowed, v2_allowed_v1_denied}
  - Metric: authz.v2.evaluator_error{service,error_kind}
  - Structured log event (debug-level): authz_dual_read_comparison with request_id, subject_type, action, scope, v1_decision, v2_decision, denial_code (when available)
- Adds a common "decision wrapper" type in the authz library to preserve denial metadata without changing existing caller logic.
- Implements dual-read hooks at both enforcement layers:
  - API Gateway middleware: runs v1 check; optionally runs v2 evaluation in parallel and records mismatch; never blocks on v2 in dual-read mode.
  - Control Plane service interceptor: same behavior; ensures internal service-to-service calls also report comparisons.
- Adds dashboards + runbook pointers (internal links) to ensure the rollout is observable.

Design notes
- Determinism: comparisons are computed from the same normalized input (subject, action, scope) that the caller uses for enforcement. This PR adds a small normalizer to avoid "false mismatches" due to scope serialization differences.
- Performance: v2 evaluation is run concurrently with the v1 check when dual-read is enabled, and is sampled in production by default. We also cap time spent on v2 evaluation (timeout = 30ms at gateway, 50ms in control plane) to avoid tail latency regressions.
- Safety: any v2 evaluator error in dual-read mode is treated as "comparison skipped" (fail-open) and emitted as a metric; no customer-facing behavior change.
- Enforce flag is wired but guarded: rbac_v2.enforce requires rbac_v2.enabled and will refuse to start if enabled without an allowlist in prod (prevent accidental global enablement).

Changed areas (high level)
- services/api-gateway
  - Adds rbac_v2 flag plumbing
  - Adds dual-read evaluation in authz middleware (v1 enforced)
  - Adds metrics + structured logging
- services/control-plane
  - Adds server interceptor support for dual-read
  - Adds normalizer for scope and action strings
- libs/authz
  - Adds FeatureFlagProvider interface + default implementation
  - Adds DualReadComparator and Decision types
  - Adds Prometheus metric definitions
- ops/observability
  - Adds Grafana dashboard JSON and alert draft (mismatch spike / v2 error spike)

Testing
- Unit tests for feature flag parsing + precedence
- Unit tests for dual-read mismatch accounting and error handling
- Integration-ish test in gateway package to assert:
  - v1 decision is enforced
  - v2 evaluator errors do not change response
  - mismatch metric increments correctly

Rollout plan (follow-up)
1) Enable rbac_v2.enabled in staging and internal dev
2) Enable rbac_v2.dual_read at 100% sampling in staging
3) Enable dual-read at 10% sampling in prod (hosted) with dashboards
4) After permission inventory + policy bundle stability, start allowlisted enforcement trials with rbac_v2.enforce (separate change control)

Open questions
- Whether to expose mismatch details in a debug header for internal admins only (not included here; see discussion below)

Links
- Linear: ENG-18388
- Related PRs: gateway deny-by-default middleware (pr-28159), policy evaluator client w caching (pr-28203)

Checklist
- [x] No behavior change when flags are off
- [x] Dual-read mode is fail-open on v2 errors/timeouts
- [x] Metrics emitted for total, mismatches, evaluator errors
- [x] Sampling supported to bound overhead
- [x] Added tests + updated internal dashboard assets
- [x] Added release guardrails to prevent accidental global enforce in prod

- Enforce flag is wired but guarded: rbac_v2.enforce requires rbac_v2.enabled and will refuse to start if enabled without an allowlist in prod (prevent accidental global enablement).
